title: The Hidden Meaning of Stress
description: Identify the implied meaning based on which word is stressed.

questions.question: "I didn't say HE stole the money." — What does the speaker imply?
questions.explanation: Stressing "HE" implies: I said someone else did it, not him specifically.

questions.question: "I didn't say he STOLE the money." — What does the speaker imply?
options: He took it, but in a different way (borrowed, found, etc.)
questions.explanation: Stressing "STOLE" implies: he took the money, but maybe borrowed or found it — not necessarily stole.

questions.question: "I didn't say he stole the MONEY." — What does the speaker imply?
options: He stole something else, not money
questions.explanation: Stressing "MONEY" implies: he stole something, but it wasn't the money — it was something else.

questions.question: The speaker says "Really?" with RISING intonation. They are expressing ___.
questions.explanation: Rising intonation on "Really?" signals genuine surprise or disbelief.

questions.question: "We MUST act NOW." — This persuasive pattern uses...
options: Rising stress on key words for emphasis
questions.explanation: Persuasive speech uses strong stress on action words (MUST, NOW) to create urgency.

questions.question: "I woulda helped" is the reduced form of "I ___ have helped."
questions.explanation: woulda = would have. Very common in casual American English.

questions.question: "You shoulda told me earlier." "Shoulda" is the reduction of...
questions.explanation: shoulda = should have. Used in informal speech to express regret.

questions.question: Which English variety is known for NOT pronouncing the final "r" in "car"?
questions.explanation: British RP is non-rhotic: "car" = /kɑː/ without the final /r/ sound.

questions.question: Australian English is known for...
options: Rising intonation at the end of statements (sounds like questions)
questions.explanation: Australian English often uses rising intonation on statements, which can sound like questions to others.

questions.question: The principle of "intelligibility" means...
options: You must have a perfect American accent
Your goal is to be clearly understood, not accent-free
questions.explanation: Intelligibility = being understood clearly. Your accent is part of your identity.

title: Reducciones Avanzadas
description: Recognizes advanced reductions of connected natural speech.

cards.back: = would have. Reduction of auxiliary + have. It is pronounced as a single word.
cards.example: I woulda helped if I'd known. (= I would have helped if I had known.)

cards.back: = should have. Express regret. Very common in informal speech.
cards.example: You shoulda told me earlier. (= You should have told me earlier.)

cards.back: = could have. Possibility not realized in the past.
cards.example: We coulda won the game. (= We could have won the game.)

cards.back: = must have. Deduction about the past.
cards.example: He musta forgotten about the meeting. (= He must have forgotten.)

cards.back: = might have. Uncertain possibility about the past.
cards.example: She mighta left already. (= She might have left already.)

cards.back: = wouldn't have. Negative of would have.
cards.example: I wouldn'ta done that. (= I wouldn't have done that.)
You oughta see a doctor. (= You ought to see a doctor.)

cards.back: = supposed to. What is expected or should happen.
cards.example: You're supposta be here at 9. (= You're supposed to be here at 9.)
I useta live in New York. (= I used to live in New York.)
There's lotsa things to do here. (= There are lots of things to do.)

title: Acento Global
description: Match each pronunciation feature to the English variant.

title: Persuasive Intonation
description: Build persuasive sentences with key emphasis words.

sentences.prompt: Build a persuasive statement about climate action
sentences.correct: We MUST act NOW to protect our future.

sentences.prompt: Build an emphatic statement about teamwork
Build a persuasive call to action
sentences.correct: The TIME for change is NOW, not tomorrow.
I did NOT say that we should give up.

sentences.prompt: Build a statement stressing the subject
sentences.correct: SHE was the one who solved the problem.
Well... I'm not entirely sure about that approach.

title: Advanced Pronunciation Vocabulary
description: Decipher terms about intonation, emphasis and rhythm.

words.hint: Intonation; rises and falls of voice
Emphasis; give importance to a word

title: The Hidden Meaning of Stress Listening

options: We coulda won the game. (= We could have won the game.)
She mighta left already. (= She might have left already.)
I wouldn'ta done that. (= I wouldn't have done that.)
You oughta see a doctor. (= You ought to see a doctor.)
